name: Confirm you want to delete this notification
pageTitle: Delete - Import and export applications - GOV.UK
caption: CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017
heading: Confirm you want to delete this notification
bodyCopy: You will not be able to undo this action.
It will take up to 1 minute for the action to take effect. Refresh your dashboard page to see the change to this notification's status.
backLink: Back (a#back-link, class="govuk-back-link back-link--next-to-breadcrumbs", href="#" — a real govuk-back-link component, but carrying a custom modifier class and rendered OUTSIDE main in a custom div.navigation-links bar alongside a "Dashboard" breadcrumb link, with a JS-driven history-back handler rather than a server-known href)
postSubmitRedirect: /notification/vnet/protected/notifications (the notification dashboard listing) — the POST to /delete redirects straight to the dashboard. confirmed: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 log ('navigated to "https://importnotification-static-snd.azure.defra.cloud/notification/vnet/protected/notifications"'). No interstitial 'notification deleted' confirmation page is rendered, and no success banner was observed on the destination.
secondaryActions: Do not delete notification (link, href=/notification/vnet/protected/notifications/CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017/review?source=dashboard — returns to the review page for the notification)

govukComponents.usage: The primary destructive action — submits the POST to /delete. Uses the warning (red) variant, correct for a destructive confirm. data-prevent-double-click="true" guards against double submission.
govukComponents.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (locator resolved to <button ... class="govuk-button govuk-button--warning">). CORROBORATED by page object: the button is addressed by accessible role+name — page-objects/notification/DeleteNotificationPage.ts:6-8 (`getByRole('button', { name: 'Delete notification' })`), so the accessible name 'Delete notification' is a contract the tests depend on. RE-VERIFIED 2026-07-17: DeleteNotificationPage.ts is 9 lines and the button is its ONLY member — the page object exposes no other control, corroborating the traces' finding that this page has no user-input fields.

govukComponents.usage: The 'Back' control. It IS the govuk-back-link component, but is rendered outside <main> in a custom div.navigation-links bar next to the breadcrumbs, and its href is "#" (JS history-back).
govukComponents.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (before snapshot, eval: `Back`; ancestry eval returned OUTSIDE main, chain DIV.navigation-links__left-block < DIV.navigation-links < DIV.govuk-width-container app-width-container--wide). NOT exercised by any test — no page object exposes a back-link locator for this page.

govukComponents.usage: The cancel path — "Do not delete notification" returns to the notification's review page. Rendered as a link below the button, per the GOV.UK destructive-confirmation pattern.
govukComponents.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (before snapshot, main outerHTML). NOT exercised by any test — DeleteNotificationPage.ts exposes only btnDeleteNotification (page-objects/notification/DeleteNotificationPage.ts:6-8); no page object or spec references the cancel link. RE-VERIFIED 2026-07-17: `grep -rni 'dont-delete|do not delete|donotdelete'` across page-objects/, tests/ and workflows/ returns ZERO hits. Rendered-only evidence.

govukComponents.usage: Two paragraphs inside the inset text, plus the paragraph wrapping the cancel link.

nonStandardPatterns.whatItDoes: The section caption (the CHED reference, CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017) is emitted after the H1 in the DOM, so it displays underneath the heading rather than above it.
nonStandardPatterns.concern: Inverted from the GOV.UK Design System pattern, where the caption precedes the heading and is usually nested inside it (or immediately before it) so screen readers announce the section context as part of the heading. Here the caption is a sibling that follows the H1, so the reference is announced after the question. This is a govuk component used in a non-standard way rather than custom markup — cheap to correct in the rebuild.
nonStandardPatterns.govukAlternative:
Standard caption-above-heading: CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017

Confirm you want to delete this notification

, or the heading-with-caption variant.
nonStandardPatterns.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (main outerHTML — h1#page-primary-title precedes span.govuk-caption-xl)

nonStandardPatterns.whatItDoes: The two consequence paragraphs ('You will not be able to undo this action.' and the propagation-delay note) sit in an inset text block.
nonStandardPatterns.concern: Not strictly non-standard — inset text is a legitimate component — but for irreversible destructive consequences the Design System's Warning text component (with its ! icon and 'Warning' visually-hidden prefix) carries more semantic weight. Worth a design decision in the rebuild rather than a straight copy.
nonStandardPatterns.govukAlternative: Warning text (govuk-warning-text) for the irreversibility line; plain govuk-body for the propagation-delay note.

nonStandardPatterns.pattern: govuk-back-link with href="#", a custom modifier class, rendered outside <main> in a custom navigation bar
nonStandardPatterns.whatItDoes: A 'Back' control sits in a custom div.navigation-links bar above main content, next to a 'Dashboard' breadcrumb. It uses the real govuk-back-link class plus a custom back-link--next-to-breadcrumbs modifier, and its href is "#" with a JS history-back handler.
nonStandardPatterns.concern: The component itself is the correct govuk one — the deviations are (a) a custom non-govuk modifier class, (b) placement outside main in a bespoke navigation bar rather than directly above main content, and (c) href="#", which is a no-op without JavaScript and so fails progressive enhancement. (c) is the material defect: the new app should render a real server-known href.
nonStandardPatterns.govukAlternative: Back link (govuk-back-link) placed directly above main content with a real href and no custom modifier class.
nonStandardPatterns.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (before snapshot, eval of the anchor outerHTML and its ancestor chain)

Not referenced by any page object or spec — grep for 'crumb' across page-objects/, tests/ and workflows/ returns nothing; the tests submit it implicitly by clicking the real button. LEGACY-CORROBORATED: the field is a static hidden input in the template — `<input name="crumb" type="hidden" value="{{crumb}}"/>` (ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/views/importer/delete.html:20), always emitted inside the form. legacy.
fields.conditionalOn: Always present — hidden CSRF field inside the delete form.

fields.name: etag
fields.label: (no visible label — hidden input)
fields.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99 (control inventory eval + main outerHTML). Optimistic-concurrency token echoed back on POST so the server can reject a delete against a stale version of the notification. Not referenced by any page object or spec — grep for 'etag' across page-objects/, tests/ and workflows/ returns nothing, so no test ever exercises the stale-token branch. LEGACY-CORROBORATED: static hidden input in the template — `<input name="etag" type="hidden" value="{{etag}}">` (ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/views/importer/delete.html:21). The POST handler passes the whole payload (incl. etag) straight into `patchUtils.doPatchOperations(...)` with no field validation (routes/handlers/importer/delete.js:35-42); there is NO stale-etag error copy anywhere — grep for 'etag|concurrency|stale|conflict' in ValidationMessages.properties returns zero hits, so a stale-token rejection surfaces as a patch/HTTP failure, not an on-page validation message. legacy.
fields.conditionalOn: Always present — hidden field inside the delete form.

fields.name: save-button
fields.label: Delete notification
fields.evidence: trace fe6d46dd47acde1900760ac281d272ab2cd841c9, action 99. type=submit, id=delete-notification, name=save-button, value="Delete notification" — the value is submitted as a form field. CORROBORATED by page object page-objects/notification/DeleteNotificationPage.ts:6-8 and driven by tests/notification/ched-pp/ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:111. LEGACY-CORROBORATED: the button in the template carries exactly these attributes — `name="save-button" type="submit" value="Delete notification" id="delete-notification" data-module="govuk-button" data-prevent-double-click=true` with class `govuk-button govuk-button--warning` and visible text 'Delete notification' (ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/views/importer/delete.html:28-30). legacy.

validationMessagesLegacyNote: CONFIRMED EMPTY by the authoritative legacy source 2026-07-17. This page carries no user-input fields, and a targeted grep of the microservice ValidationMessages.properties for 'etag|concurrency|stale|conflict|delete' returns ZERO hits (ipaffs-notification-microservice/service/src/main/resources/ValidationMessages.properties). The POST handler passes the payload (crumb + etag) straight into patchUtils.doPatchOperations with no field-level validation (delete.js:35-42), and the frontend template renders {{> common/validationSummary}} but it is only ever populated with the empty errors:[] the GET handler seeds (delete.js:22). So a stale-etag or already-deleted rejection surfaces as an upstream patch/HTTP failure, NOT as on-page GOV.UK validation copy. There is genuinely no error message to mine for this page — the empty validationMessages array is a legacy-confirmed fact, not an unmined gap. confidence: legacy (ValidationMessages.properties grep; delete.js:22,35-42).
openQuestions: This page has NO user-input fields — it is a pure confirm/cancel step. There is therefore no client- or server-side validation to capture, and no govuk-error-summary was present in the snapshot (verified: querySelectorAll('.govuk-error-summary').length === 0 at action 99). Whether an error state exists at all (e.g. the etag concurrency check failing because the notification was modified or already deleted in another session) is UNKNOWN — no trace in the corpus exercised it. CORROBORATED AS A GAP by the second source: no spec asserts any error message for this page, and no page object exposes an error locator (DeleteNotificationPage.ts is 9 lines and exposes only the button). If the server rejects a stale etag, the resulting error page/copy is an unmined GAP and needs a human or a fresh test to establish.

CORRECTION 2026-07-17 — the previous version of this spec concluded that the 'up to 1 minute' copy was 'at odds with observed behaviour', reasoning that the delete test re-searches and asserts the notification is gone 'with no wait or retry'. THAT REASONING WAS WRONG ON BOTH LEGS, and the corroboration pass reverses the conclusion. (a) `expect(...).not.toBeVisible()` is a Playwright web-first assertion and DOES auto-retry — up to the configured expect timeout of 5s (playwright.config.ts:34). So the test tolerates a delay; it does not demand immediacy. (b) More decisively, the QA repo contains a purpose-built eventual-consistency helper for exactly this: NotificationDashboardPage.expectNotificationNotVisible() -> pollForNotification() re-searches the dashboard in a loop under `toPass({ timeout: 120000 })` (page-objects/notification/NotificationDashboardPage.ts:117-126) — a 120-second budget, i.e. deliberate headroom over the 'up to 1 minute' copy. The sibling openNotificationDetails() (NotificationDashboardPage.ts:163-168) inherits the same 120000ms default by delegating to the private pollDashboardAction() helper, whose `options.timeoutMs ?? 120000` default sits at NotificationDashboardPage.ts:138. (CITATION CORRECTED 2026-07-17 by adversarial re-check: the previous version cited ':138-140' as if that WERE openNotificationDetails; those lines are actually inside pollDashboardAction. The substantive claim — same 120s budget — is unaffected and holds.) The QA team therefore treats this dashboard as EVENTUALLY CONSISTENT by design, which CORROBORATES the '1 minute' copy rather than contradicting it. The delete tests simply do not use that helper — they hand-roll search + a 5s assertion (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:113-116), while notification-visibility.spec.ts:55,83,144,173,179 does use it. Read that as an under-hardened test that passes on a fast environment (and is retried once locally / twice in CI — playwright.config.ts:21), NOT as evidence the copy is stale. Confidence: inferred (file:line above). The genuine open questions for the rebuild remain: (a) what is the post-delete status value, and is the record retrievable/auditable afterwards? (b) what is the ACTUAL propagation SLA, and is '1 minute' the right number to promise?

The delete is soft, not hard, and is asynchronous. The copy says 'It will take up to 1 minute for the action to take effect' and 'see the change to this notification's status' — i.e. the record persists with a changed status rather than being removed. Yet the dashboard's default listing drops it (the test asserts view-details-<ref> is not visible; the locator is `[id="view-details-${referenceNumber}"]`, NotificationDashboardPage.ts:104-106). So 'status change' and 'disappears from the dashboard' are both true: the default dashboard filter evidently excludes the deleted status. The rebuild needs to know the post-delete status value and which listings still surface it. GAP — neither source establishes the status value itself.
The POST target is a RELATIVE form action (action="delete"), resolving against the current URL. Fragile — noted for the rebuild, which should use an absolute path.

The delete POST redirects to the dashboard with NO confirmation of success — no interstitial 'notification deleted' page and no success banner was observed on the destination (action 99 navigated straight to /notification/vnet/protected/notifications). Combined with the 'up to 1 minute' copy, a user has no positive feedback that the delete worked. The rebuild should decide whether to add a confirmation page or a success notification banner on the dashboard. Note the absence of a banner is only weakly evidenced — the trace's next action (100) re-navigates to the dashboard, so any flash message rendered on the redirect target was not snapshotted before being replaced. Treat 'no success banner' as INFERRED, not confirmed. The second source cannot settle it either: no spec asserts a success message after delete (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:113-116 goes straight to re-search).

DISCREPANCY (minor, path-prefix) 2026-07-17 — the cancel link's href differs between the rendered trace and the legacy template SOURCE, but only by the deployment base path. Rendered (confirmed, kept): /notification/vnet/protected/notifications/CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017/review?source=dashboard. Legacy template source: href="/protected/notifications/{{referenceNumber}}/review?source=dashboard" (delete.html:34-36), and the handler builds the SAME shape via constants.IMPORTER_REVIEW_PAGE(referenceNumber) + '?source=dashboard' (delete.js:44-46). The '/notification/vnet' segment is the deployment mount prefix the served app prepends, absent from the raw template path — a base-path artefact, not a semantic conflict. The '?source=dashboard' query and the /review target agree across both sources. Rendered value wins and stays confirmed; recorded here as a template-vs-rendered divergence for Reconcile.

The cancel link hardcodes ?source=dashboard in its return URL. It is unclear whether this page can be reached from anywhere other than the dashboard (e.g. directly from the review page or a deep link), and if so whether the cancel destination adapts. In the observed trace the entry path was: dashboard -> search by CHED reference -> view-details -> 'Delete notification' link (action 98, href=/notification/vnet/protected/notifications/CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017/delete) -> this page. CORROBORATED: the test drives the identical single entry path (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:105-110 — dashboard goTo, search, openNotificationDetails, then viewDetails.linkDeleteNotification.click(); the link itself is defined at page-objects/notification/ViewDetailsPage.ts:6-8 as getByRole('link', { name: 'Delete notification' })). Only that one entry path is evidenced by EITHER source, so ?source=dashboard may well be the only value ever produced — or there may be an unexercised alternate entry the rebuild needs to know about.

NO TEST EVER EXERCISES THE CANCEL PATH. 'Do not delete notification' is rendered (trace fe6d46dd, action 99) but DeleteNotificationPage.ts exposes only btnDeleteNotification — there is no locator for the cancel link anywhere in page-objects/, and no spec clicks it (re-verified 2026-07-17: grep -rni for 'dont-delete|do not delete|donotdelete' across page-objects/, tests/, workflows/ returns zero hits). So the cancel destination is evidenced ONLY by the rendered href, never by an actual navigation. The rebuild should not assume the cancel link works as its href suggests; it is untested surface in the legacy app. The same applies to the Back link — no page object addresses it either.

THIS PAGE IS NOT CHED-PP-SPECIFIC — it is shared across all four CHED types. The same DeleteNotificationPage page object is driven by all four manipulation specs: ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:111, ched-p-manipulation.spec.ts:22, ched-d-manipulation.spec.ts:22, ched-a-manipulation.spec.ts:23, each following an identical create -> search -> view details -> delete -> assert-gone shape (re-verified 2026-07-17; ched-a-manipulation.spec.ts:10-29 read in full and is line-for-line the same journey as the CHED-PP one bar the create-workflow call and the reference pattern). Only the caption (the CHED reference) varies. For the rebuild this means the delete-confirm page carries no CHED-PP-specific requirement at all, and the CHED-PP app can own a simple local version rather than inheriting a shared one. Confidence: inferred (cross-type behaviour was not traced here, only the CHED-PP instance was; the shared page object plus the four identical specs are the evidence).

THIS PAGE HAS NO ACCESSIBILITY COVERAGE. grep -i for 'delete' across tests/accessibility/ returns nothing (re-verified 2026-07-17) — the delete-confirm page is absent from the accessibility suite that otherwise names every page in journey order. Given the caption-after-h1 defect recorded in nonStandardPatterns (which an axe scan would likely not catch, but a manual review would), and the href="#" back link (which axe WOULD flag), this is an untested a11y surface in the legacy app. The rebuild should include it in the a11y suite from the start.

AUTHORISATION IS UNTESTED BEYOND ONE ROLE. Every delete test runs as a single persona — `test.use({ storageState: AUTH_FILES.b2cNotifier })` (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:10, ched-a-manipulation.spec.ts:9) with `users.b2cNotifier.role` passed to every dashboard goTo (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:99,105,113; ched-a-manipulation.spec.ts:11,17,25) — line ranges tightened 2026-07-17 by adversarial re-check; the previous ':9-10'/':9-11' ranges did not actually span the `users.b2cNotifier.role` usage they cited. b2cNotifier is the notification's own creator in each case (the test creates it, then deletes it). The repo has other personas — notification-visibility.spec.ts exercises b2bInspector, agent and b2bAdmin against dashboards (notification-visibility.spec.ts:43-186). So: who is ALLOWED to delete a notification? Can an agent delete a notifier's notification, or a b2bAdmin delete either's? Can anyone delete a notification they did not create? NEITHER source answers this — no trace and no test covers a non-creator delete, and no test asserts a 403/permission-denied path. This is a security-relevant GAP and a real question for the rebuild, which must decide the authorisation rule deliberately rather than inherit an unexamined one. Confidence: gap. LEGACY INPUT 2026-07-17 (partial — does not close the gap, but constrains it): the legacy app applies NO role- or ownership-based authorisation at this route. Both the GET and POST are registered with the generic `userAccess` / `userAccessPostRequest` options (routes/routes.js:830-838), i.e. any authenticated user, not a role-scoped or owner-scoped policy. The ONLY server-side gate is `isDeletable(status, type)` (delete.js:15-17), which is a STATUS+TYPE check, not a WHO check — it never consults the notification's owner or the caller's role. So in the legacy system, whether a non-creator (agent, b2bAdmin, another notifier) can delete a notification is governed entirely by whatever upstream ownership scoping the notification-fetch (`notificationUtils.getNotification`) applies, NOT by anything on this page's handler. The rebuild must NOT read this as 'anyone can delete anything'; it means the delete-confirm handler itself is authorisation-agnostic and the real ownership rule lives upstream (unmined here). confidence: legacy for the route-options + isDeletable facts (routes.js:830-838; delete.js:15-17); gap for the effective ownership rule.

Journey position is -1 (post-submission, off the main creation flow). This page was only ever observed against a SUBMITTED notification (CHEDPP.GB.2026.1526017). Every delete test in the QA repo creates and SUBMITS a notification first (ched-pp-manipulation.spec.ts:100-101 createChedPPNotification then asserts a confirmation reference number) before deleting it — no test deletes a draft/in-progress notification. LEGACY RESOLUTION 2026-07-17 — the trace/test GAP is now CLOSED by the authoritative source: a DRAFT notification DOES reach this same delete page and copy. The GET handler gates entry on `isDeletable(notification.status, notification.type)` and for a CHED-PP (non-IMP) type that returns true for exactly [AMEND, DRAFT, SUBMITTED, NEW] (ipaffs-frontend-notification/service/src/helpers/actionable_notification_status.js:31-39). DRAFT is in that set, so drafts use the identical /protected/notifications/{referenceNumber}/delete route, template and copy — there is NO separate draft-delete route. Any status OUTSIDE that set (e.g. VALIDATED, REJECTED, CANCELLED, REPLACED, DELETED, IN_PROGRESS, MODIFY, SPLIT_CONSIGNMENT, PARTIALLY_REJECTED) causes the GET to fall through to the 404 handler (delete.js:15-17) rather than render this page. confidence: legacy (actionable_notification_status.js:31-39; delete.js:15-17). NOTE for the rebuild: this deletable-status set is legacy POLICY, not an inviolable law — the rebuild should confirm which live-animals statuses are deletable rather than inherit [AMEND, DRAFT, SUBMITTED, NEW] verbatim.

No page-level confirmation of what is being deleted beyond the bare CHED reference in the caption — no summary of commodity, importer or arrival date. The rebuild may want richer confirmation detail, but that is a design decision, not an observed requirement.

SCOPE NOTE on the fields list: the action-99 control inventory also returned cookie-banner controls (button#accept-cookies, button#button-cookies, and hidden inputs crumb/returnUrl/fromFooterHeader belonging to the cookie-banner form). These are site-wide chrome from the IPAFFS page template, not requirements of this page, and are deliberately excluded from `fields`. Worth knowing only that the cookie banner was still displayed to this authenticated user mid-journey.

CORROBORATION RESULT — NO NEW FIELDS FOUND. The second source raised the field count by zero: DeleteNotificationPage.ts exposes exactly one member (the button), and no workflow or config in the QA repo touches this page (`grep -rn 'delete' workflows/notification/ched-pp-workflows.ts` returns zero hits, so ChedPPNotificationConfig / DEFAULT_CONFIG carry no delete-related entry). EVIDENCE CORRECTED 2026-07-17 by adversarial re-check: the previous version claimed "grep for 'delete' across workflows/ returns zero hits", which is FALSE — `grep -rni 'delete'` across workflows/ returns three hits (workflows/address-book/address-book-workflows.ts:71,75,76 — deleteAddress / viewAddress.deleteAddress() / viewAddress.confirmDelete()). Those are ADDRESS-BOOK deletes on a different page object entirely, so the conclusion (no workflow drives the notification-delete page) survives intact; only the overstated grep scope was wrong. Worth knowing for the rebuild that a SEPARATE delete-confirm flow exists for address book, which may or may not share copy/markup with this one — not mined here. That is a genuine agreement between the two sources rather than a hole: this page really is a bare confirm/cancel step with three hidden/implicit form fields and no user input. The lower bound and the upper bound meet here — unusual for this corpus and worth stating explicitly.
